topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed with a title that made my stomach clench: *The Obsidian Heart*. I hadn't seen it, not really, until I found the scattered manuscript pages tucked beneath the keyboard. My hands, slick with sudden, cold sweat, gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. I stared at the meticulously crafted prose, the intimate details—my morning coffee ritual, the way I hum off-key when I’m concentrating—rendered with a terrifying, clinical accuracy. A slow, deep tremor started in my chest, a vibration that threatened to shake the whole room. I couldn't breathe. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "What is this?" My voice came out tight, thin, and dangerously low. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of bewildered apology, and the sheer audacity of it made my jaw lock so hard I could taste copper.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked inside a rarely used drawer—a sheaf of typed pages, undeniably a novel. Every sentence was a mirror, reflecting moments I hadn't even consciously registered as significant. A furious heat began to bloom behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my temples that made my vision swim slightly. I snatched the papers, my movements jerky and sharp. I didn't bother reading the next chapter; the intent was already clear. I threw the stack onto the coffee table, the sound sharp in the silence of the apartment. "Stop," I hissed, the word barely escaping my throat, vibrating with a raw, contained fury. He flinched, looking genuinely wounded, and that vulnerability only fueled the tight, constricted feeling in my chest. I paced three steps, then stopped dead, my breathing shallow and fast, every muscle coiled tight, ready to spring.

[story 3]

It was the casual discovery that shattered the air. A draft of his writing, hidden in an old folder on his desktop, revealed a sprawling narrative centered entirely on my life. A sudden, violent surge of internal tension seized me, making my vision narrow. I felt a profound sense of betrayal wash over me, cold and sharp, like shattered glass against my skin. I walked over to him, my shadow falling across the screen, and the air around us seemed to thicken with unspoken accusation. "You wrote this?" The question wasn't asked; it was spat out, laced with a tone so brittle it felt like it might snap. I watched his composure crumble, his face draining of color, and the sheer, unwarranted self-absorption of it all made me want to scream until my lungs gave out.
